perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” m eans all confidential and proprietary information disclosed to or obtained by the Receiving Party or its Representatives from the Disclosing Party or its Representatives, including without limitation, information relating to: proprietary technology, produc ts, proprietary plans, services, computer programs and processes; data and source code; any inventions and associated information, manuals, plans, drawings, blueprints or schematics; information relating to CWF0 001 Mutual Non -Disclosure Agreement C.W. Fletcher & Sons Ltd employees and customers of the Disclosing Party; promotional and marketing information and opportunities; financial information; trade secrets; any and all information that a Receiving Party may obtain on a walk -through examination of the Disclosing Party’s and/or its clients’ premises; the existence of discussions between the Parties regarding the Purpose, or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect thereto (including the status of such discussions); and all information, of any nature whatsoever, disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the R eceiving Party in connection with the Purpose, whether before or after the date of this Agreement. In each case such information may be contained in tangible materials including, but not limited to writings, drawings, models, prototypes, test specimens, pr oduction units, data specifications, reports, analysis, methods, compilations and computer programs, or may be in the nature of verbally or visually communicated intellectual knowledge.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” m eans some information, as determined solely by the Disclosing Party, that is explicitly marked as such and provided to the Receiving Party or its Representatives from the Disclosing Party or its Representatives. This includes only final product designs, client lists, and marketing strategies when delivered in a sealed envelope.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of "Confidential Information" drastically narrows the scope of what is protected, contradicting the original broad definition. The added stipulation that information is only confidential if explicitly marked so and provided in a specific manner introduces ambiguity and undermines the principle of protecting sensitive information. It severely limits the scope of protected information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets (United States - Uniform Trade Secrets Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the broad scope of trade secret protection under the UTSA, which includes a wide array of confidential business information, not just explicitly marked information or information delivered in a specific manner. The UTSA protects information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable, and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text severely restricts the scope of "Confidential Information" compared to the original contract, by only including information explicitly marked and delivered in a sealed envelope. The Law Explanation states that the UTSA protects a wider array of confidential business information. This change introduces a significant deviation from the typical understanding and protection of trade secrets, potentially undermining the Disclosing Party's ability to safeguard its sensitive information. The scraped snippets do not directly define what constitutes a trade secret, but the Law Explanation, referring to the UTSA, indicates that trade secret protection is broader than the modified definition allows. This creates a legal contradiction under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the new definition does not align with standard legal interpretations, specifically the UTSA.

perturbation.original_text: “Representatives” means the employees, Affiliates, agents, contractors, consultants, representatives and pr ofessional advisors of a Party.
perturbation.changed_text: “Representatives” means only full-time employees of a Party directly involved in the Purpose and who have signed a separate confidentiality agreement with the Party.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of "Representatives" significantly limits the scope of who is considered a representative of a party, excluding agents, contractors, consultants, affiliates and other professional advisors. This may leave the disclosing party liable for the actions of these excluded groups. This change creates uncertainty about the extent of responsibility and liability for actions of individuals acting on behalf of each party.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modified definition contradicts the established principles of agency law, which defines an agent broadly as someone who acts on behalf of a principal, encompassing a wider range of actors than just full-time employees. By narrowing the definition, the contract may fail to hold a party responsible for the actions of those who would legally be considered their agents.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts agency law. The original text's broad definition of 'Representatives' included agents, contractors, and consultants. The changed text limits 'Representatives' to only full-time employees, excluding those other roles. This contradicts agency law, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states, 'A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal.' By excluding agents, contractors, and consultants from the definition of 'Representatives,' the contract fails to hold a party responsible for the actions of those who legally would be considered their agents, which directly contradicts the law.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of England and the Parties agree to sub mit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts for any and all disputes, claims and indifference arising out of, or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be governed by the principles of equity and good faith, and any disputes shall be resolved through informal mediation, without recourse to any legal jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: The modified clause replaces a clear choice of law and jurisdiction (England) with vague references to "principles of equity and good faith" and informal mediation. This creates uncertainty about which legal standards apply and eliminates the right to pursue legal action in a court of law, which is a fundamental aspect of contract enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law and Forum Selection
perturbation.law_citation: Erie R. Co. v. Tompkins, 304 U.S. 64 (1938)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified clause contradicts the legal principles concerning choice of law and forum selection, particularly relevant under the Erie Doctrine in the United States. While parties can agree on choice of law, replacing it with vague principles and removing the right to legal recourse makes the agreement unenforceable, as it fails to provide a clear framework for resolving disputes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Erie doctrine | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Erie doctrine The Erie doctrine is a binding principle where federal courts exercising diversity jurisdiction apply federal procedural law of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure , but must also apply state substantive law . Pre-Erie Doctrine: The Erie doctrine derives from the landmark 1938 U.S. Supreme Court case, Erie Railroad Co. v. Tompkins (1938). The Rules Decision Act of 1789, codified as 28 U.S.C. § 1652 , laid the foundation for how federal courts should operate when exercising diversity jurisdiction, and provided that the “laws of the several states” apply in federal court. Prior to Erie , federal courts followed Swift v. Tyson (1842), which interpreted the “laws of the several states” to include only state statutes and local custom, and not the state common law . This meant that federal courts were free to ignore state substantive law established by common law through that state’s judiciary when exercising diversity jurisdiction and could apply what they saw as the true general common law. In Swift , for example, the Court disregarded New York commercial law established by the state judicial precedent, and instead saw its role as “express[ing] our own opinion of the true result of the commercial law upon the question.” The proper law to apply, the Court believed, “may be truly declared in the languages of Cicero, adopted by Lord Mansfield in Luke v. Lyde , to be in a great measure not the law of a single country only, but of the commercial world.” This reasoning reflects the view that there was one true and accurate body of laws that can be ascertained regardless of a polity’s laws, i.e. natural law . Erie Railroad Co. v. Tompkins The U.S. Supreme Court in Erie Railroad Co. v. Tompkins , is an opinion by Justice Brandeis, departed from Swift and held that the language in the Rules Decision Act stating that federal courts when exercising diversity jurisdiction shall apply the “laws of the several states” includes state common law. Specifically, in Erie , Tompkins lost his arm while walking on a footpath alongside a railroad track when a train car’s door came loose and injured him. Under Pennsylvania state common law, Tompkins was a trespasser on the railroad’s property and could not recover, but under the general common law he was not a tres
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts established legal principles by replacing a clear choice of law and jurisdiction (England) with vague references to "principles of equity and good faith" and informal mediation. This substitution eliminates the certainty required for contract enforcement. While the Erie Doctrine primarily concerns federal courts in the US, the core issue it addresses—the necessity of a defined legal framework—is relevant here. The original text provided a clear framework (English law and courts), whereas the altered text provides none, rendering the agreement unenforceable due to the lack of a mechanism for resolving disputes. The 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition matches, as the modified text deviates significantly from legally recognized frameworks for dispute resolution.

perturbation.original_text: The confidentiality obl igations set out in this Agreement will survive expiry and/or termination of this Agreement for a period of one (1) yea r.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidentiality obligations set out in this Agreement will terminate immediately upon the earlier of the agreement's termination or either party's request.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a one-year survival period for confidentiality obligations after the agreement ends. The modified text eliminates this survival period, meaning that all confidential information is no longer protected once the agreement is terminated or if a party requests termination of the confidentiality, making proprietary assets vulnerable.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification potentially contradicts trade secret protection laws. Under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) 18 U.S. Code § 1836, misappropriation can occur even after termination of an agreement if the information still qualifies as a trade secret and reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy are made. Eliminating the survival clause may undermine the "reasonable efforts" requirement to protect trade secrets, weakening legal protections against misappropriation.

(b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the intent to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). The original text provided a one-year survival period for confidentiality, aligning with the need to maintain secrecy. The modified text eliminates this survival period, making trade secrets immediately vulnerable upon termination or a party's request. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 USC 1836) focuses on civil proceedings for misappropriation, the Law Explanation correctly states that eliminating the survival clause may undermine 'reasonable efforts' to protect trade secrets, weakening legal protections against misappropriation. The original clause provided a duration where reasonable effort was explicitly stated. The modified clause negates any such effort. This constitutes a strong legal contradiction.
